description:
Legal record (#48) in a court register/pinqas from a Rabbanite beit din in Cairo (on ff. 12v-13r-v of BL OR 6356). In Hebrew. Location: Cairo. Dated 23 Iyyar 5443 AM, which is 1683 CE. 

(Document #48) Complex and extensive dispute resolution involving the same ketubba settlement, inheritance, and parties from document #46 (PGPID 40924): Sitt al-Bayt bt. Yosef Fanji(?), widow of Shemuʾel Ṭayyib aka Ḥajj, in addition to her four living sons Yosef, Yiṣḥaq, Yaʿaqov, and Ḥayyim (the son Avraham is already deceased). The opening lines of the case explain how Sitt al-Bayt's husband left outstanding debts with an unnamed non-Jew at the time of his death, "a large sum recorded in a defter... close to 20,000 medin" that her sons had to settle/pay in court. The brothers won a second suit that was brought against them by an unnamed non-Jew, who claimed that the large apartment building in the Ṭayyib family inheritance was in fact waqf property and they were not the rightful owners. This second qadi court case cost the family 10,000 medin, which they spent on repairs to the property (asserting their ownership via upkeep) and in securing a ḥujja which verified that there was no waqfiyya document supporting any counter-claim against their rightful ownership. The brothers paid the fees for both cases with their own assets rather than by liquidating any part of their father's estate.

Folio 13r (Document #48 contd.) After settling their father's debts, one of the five Ṭayyib brothers died leaving three children and a widow Esther bt. Shalom Ṭayyib (who was likely his paternal cousin). When Sitt al-Bayt first tried to take the oath of widowhood and receive the full valuation of her ketubba (70,000 medin) a family feud unfolded between her, the four surviving sons, and her daughter-in-law Esther. Sitt al-Bayt appointed two men with power of attorney to represent her in the case: Yosef ha-Kohen and his son-in-law Natan Ben-Efrayim. The two sides reached a compromise regarding Sitt al-Bayt's ketubba valuation, by which she received 50,000 medin but relinquished any claim to the 20,000 medin worth of civet cats and properties in the Karaite quarter (possessions also mentioned in document #46). Possession over civet cats and property was divided in fifths between the four brothers and Avraham's widow Esther. The four wives and widow of Sitt al-Bayt's sons each contribute 4,000 medin from their own dowries (a combined 20,000 of 50,000) toward the compromise with their mother-in-law.

Folio 13v: (Document #48 contd.) Final lines of the dispute resolution between Sitt al-Bayt and her sons/daughters-in-law. This section includes the patronymics of Sitt al-Bayt's other four daughters-in-law: Miryam bt. Moshe Ṭayyib (Yosef's wife), Esther bt. Yehuda ha-Kohen (Yiṣḥaq's wife), Raḥma bt. David Ṭayyib (Yaʿaqov's wife), and Ḥabība/Havīva bt. Avraham [no surname listed] (Ḥayyim's wife). Given the recurrence of their uncommon surname, it is likely that three of the Ṭayyib brothers married their paternal cousins (Esther bt. Shalom Ṭayyib, Miryam, and Raḥma). MCD.
description_he: רשומה משפטית (#48) בפנקס בית דין רבני בקהיר (BL OR 6356, דפים 12v-13v); בעברית; כ"ג אייר ה'תמ"ג (1683 לספירה); יישוב סכסוך מורכב ומקיף הכולל את הסדר הכתובה, הירושה ואת אותם הצדדים מ-#46; בעל הנפטר הותיר חובות; הבנים ניצחו בתביעת וַקְף; בסופו של דבר סת אל-בית קיבלה 50,000 מֶדין; כלותיה כל אחת תרמה 4,000 מֶדין מנדוניותיהן
